one_line_takeaway: LLM agents (especially SWE-agent with Claude Sonnet 4) can cut SAST false-positive rates from ~98% to ~6% via genuine cross-file iterative reasoning (not just extra context), but the benefit is highly backbone- and CWE-dependent, weaker models can degrade via 'scope drift,' and even the best configuration silently suppresses 22% of true vulnerabilities, concentrated in cryptography/policy CWEs -- making full automation unsafe without human-in-the-loop review.
key_finding: On OWASP Benchmark, the best configuration (Claude Sonnet 4 + SWE-agent) reduces remaining FPR from an initial union FPR of 98.3% down to 6.3% (a 92.1% reduction versus vanilla prompting's 23.0% FPR for the same backbone). On Vul4J (n=50 sample), GPT-5 + Aider achieves the top F1 of 83.3% (92.6% recall, 75.8% precision), and GPT-5 configurations reach up to 93.3% FP identification recall (OpenHands). On the contamination-free OSS-Fuzz C/C++ set, full SWE-agent + Claude Sonnet 4 identifies 95.5% of FPs at 95.5% precision (F1=95.5%, accuracy=96.0%) versus 36.4% FP identification recall for vanilla prompting (F1=51.6%). Ablations show cross-file navigation is the dominant capability (removing it drops accuracy to 44.0%, F1 to 62.5%), multi-turn interaction is second (F1 drops to 56.3% without it), tool-based verification has a smaller localized effect (F1=88.9%), and config-file access is negligible (F1=97.7%, no full-correct case lost). Critically, an 'oracle-context' vanilla-LLM baseline given the exact same files the full agent accessed achieves only 36.4% recall / 51.6% F1 -- nearly identical to plain vanilla prompting -- showing the agentic advantage comes from iterative evidence-gathering/reasoning, not context volume alone. Gains are strongly backbone-dependent: DeepSeek Chat shows no net benefit from agentic scaffolding (vanilla already at 11.2% FPR on OWASP; SWE-agent trajectory audit attributes this to 'scope drift' -- 145/1,303 paired cases where added agentic exploration causes DeepSeek to over-report speculative issues like stack-trace exposure or missing cookie attributes as TPs). A separate TP-retention experiment on the 1,415 OWASP positives with the best configuration finds 22.25% of true vulnerabilities are incorrectly suppressed as FPs overall, with miss rates below 3% for injection CWEs (78, 89, 79) but 77.17% for CWE-327 (Weak Cryptography), 84.50% for CWE-328 (Weak Hashing), 77.11% for CWE-501 (Trust Boundary Violation), and 50.00% for CWE-614 (Secure Cookie Flag).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is the first systematic comparative study of three LLM agent frameworks -- Aider, OpenHands, and SWE-agent -- for filtering false positives (FPs) produced by SAST tools, evaluated against vanilla zero-shot LLM prompting across three backbone models (Claude Sonnet 4, DeepSeek Chat, GPT-5). The authors build their own evaluation pipeline: aggregating alerts from four SAST tools (CodeQL, Semgrep, SonarQube, Joern) on the OWASP Benchmark v1.2, manually triaging a sample of real-world CodeQL alerts from the Vul4J dataset, and constructing a novel, contamination-free post-cutoff C/C++ alert pool from OSS-Fuzz (mpv, libhevc, libical, gpsd, quickjs, fixing commits 10-12 months after Claude Sonnet 4's training cutoff) to test generalization. They also run capability ablations (removing multi-turn interaction, cross-file navigation, tool-based verification, config-file access) and three additional non-agentic baselines (LLM4SA-style prompting, context-augmented LLM, oracle-context LLM) to isolate the source of agentic gains, plus a qualitative trajectory analysis (256 success cases, 103 failure cases) identifying success patterns (P1-P4) and failure modes (F1-F3).
threat_model: This is not an attack/defense security paper with an adversarial threat model; it is an empirical/measurement study of an LLM-agent-based defensive tooling problem (SAST alert triage). The 'threat' being addressed is operational: SAST tools flood developers with false positives, causing alert fatigue and suppression of genuine warnings (the paper cites that 50.8% of suppressions are 'useless,' from prior work). The paper's own experiments quantify a downstream risk: aggressive agentic FP filtering can itself suppress true vulnerabilities (TP retention experiment), which the authors frame as a practical/safety trade-off rather than an adversarial threat model in the traditional security-paper sense.
limitations: The authors' own stated threats to validity: (1) LLM non-determinism across multi-step agentic reasoning, mitigated only partially by temperature=0. (2) RQ2's Vul4J sample is only 50 alerts due to manual-triage capacity, with most labels from a single annotator (first author) after patch-matching -- sample may not represent the full 3,426-alert distribution, and results can be skewed by which CodeQL rules were sampled. (3) OSS-Fuzz evaluation is a focused 50-alert validation set for the single strongest configuration only, not a comprehensive C/C++ benchmark or full cross-model/cross-agent comparison. (4) The alert-specific binary TP/FP framing can undercount cases where an agent correctly rejects the stated rationale but identifies a genuinely different real security issue in the same code -- such cases are scored as incorrect. (5) Real-world evaluation relies primarily on CodeQL alerts, though the authors partially mitigate this by showing FP overlap correlation across scanners. (6) Cost/token/round data (Table 8) show large practical overhead differences (e.g., OpenHands+Claude averaging 20.5 rounds and $0.1867/task vs Aider's single-round, $0.0469/task) that constrain real-world deployability but are not folded into the accuracy comparisons.

datasets_used: OWASP Benchmark v1.2 (2,740 Java instances, 11 CWEs, 1,415 positive / 1,325 negative)
Vul4J (79 reproducible Java vulnerabilities across 51 repositories, derived from Project KB; 3,426 CodeQL alerts, 50 sampled)
OSS-Fuzz post-cutoff C/C++ alert pool (5 projects: mpv, libhevc, libical, gpsd, quickjs; 804 eligible CodeQL alerts, 50 sampled with seed=42)
quantitative_results: OWASP: initial union FPR 98.3% (1,303/1,325 negatives flagged by >=1 SAST tool) reduced to 6.3% by SWE-agent + Claude Sonnet 4 (92.1% reduction), vs 23.0% for vanilla Claude prompting

SAST tool baselines on OWASP: CodeQL Acc 65.5%/Prec 60.3%/Rec 97.0%/F1 74.4%/FPR 68.2%; Semgrep Acc 58.9%/Prec 56.3%/Rec 90.4%/F1 69.4%/FPR 74.8%; SonarQube Acc 52.0%/Prec 51.9%/Rec 95.6%/F1 67.3%/FPR 94.6%; Joern Acc 49.1%/Prec 54.7%/Rec 8.2%/F1 14.3%/FPR 7.2%
Vul4J (n=50): GPT-5+Aider best F1 83.3% (Rec 92.6%, Prec 75.8%); OpenHands+GPT-5 highest recall 93.3% at Prec 70.0%; DeepSeek+OpenHands highest precision 100.0% but only 38.7% recall
OSS-Fuzz: SWE-agent full (Claude Sonnet 4) Acc 96.0%, Prec 95.5%, Rec 95.5%, F1 95.5% vs vanilla LLM Acc 68.0%, Prec 88.9%, Rec 36.4%, F1 51.6%
OSS-Fuzz ablations: w/o cross-file F1=62.5% (Acc 44.0%); w/o multi-turn F1=56.3%; w/o tool-based verification F1=88.9%; w/o config read F1=97.7%
OSS-Fuzz baselines: LLM4SA-style prompting F1=70.8% (Rec 77.3%); context-augmented vanilla LLM F1=66.7% (Rec 54.5%); oracle-context vanilla LLM F1=51.6% (Rec 36.4%, same as plain vanilla)

TP retention (OWASP positives, Claude+SWE-agent, 1,411/1,415 completed runs): overall 77.7% TP retention / 22.25% miss rate; per-CWE miss rates: CWE-78 2.38%, CWE-89 1.10%, CWE-79 0.41%, CWE-90 0%, CWE-643 0%, CWE-327 77.17%, CWE-328 84.50%, CWE-501 77.11%, CWE-614 50.00%

Cost (Table 8): Claude+Aider 1.0 rounds/13,321 tokens/$0.0469; Claude+OpenHands 20.5 rounds/219,299 tokens/$0.1867; Claude+SWE-agent 9.0 rounds/130,846 tokens/$0.1501; DeepSeek+Aider $0.0028 (cheapest); GPT-5+SWE-agent $0.1052

Contamination check: filename-only leakage probe accuracy at chance (Claude Sonnet 4 51.6%, GPT-5 51.0%, DeepSeek Chat 49.8%); zero trajectories (of 15,636 OWASP trajectories) contained prior-exposure language

DeepSeek scope-drift audit: 145/1,303 paired OWASP cases where vanilla was correct but SWE-agent was wrong, all ground-truth negatives flipped to TP, driven by stack-trace exposure (80), info/error disclosure (93), cookie-hardening (54), CRLF/response-splitting (23), weak crypto (35), exception-handling concerns (97)
baselines_compared: Vanilla zero-shot LLM prompting (same backbone as each agent)
LLM4SA-style prompting (reused task instruction/output format, not the full program-dependence context pipeline)
Context-augmented vanilla LLM (heuristic cross-file context: call-graph neighbors, includes, CodeQL code-flow)
Oracle-context vanilla LLM (given exact files the full agent accessed)
Four SAST tools as the pre-agent baseline: CodeQL v2.23.7, Semgrep v1.145.0, SonarQube Community v9.9.8, Joern v4.0.454
